mn31:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Nātika in the Brick Hall.
mn31:10.2: But while you are dwelling thus, heedful, ardent, and resolute, have you attained a comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state?”
mn31:10.4: Here, *bhante*, whenever we wish, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome *dhammas*, we enter and dwell in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*savitakkaṁ*] and examination [*savicāraṁ*], and has *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*].
mn31:10.5: This, *bhante*, is the comfortable abiding that we have attained, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state, while dwelling heedfully, ardently, and resolutely.”
mn31:11-13.2: But for the transcending of this abiding and for the calming of this abiding, have you attained another comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state?”
mn31:11-13.4: Here, *bhante*, whenever we wish, with the stilling of thought [*vitakkavicārānaṁ*] and examination [*vitakkavicārānaṁ*], with inner clarity, with the unification [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] of mind, without thought [*avitakkaṁ*], without examination [*avicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi* [*samādhijaṁ*], we enter and dwell in the second *jhāna*.
mn31:11-13.5: For the transcending of this abiding and for the calming of this abiding, this is another comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state, that we have attained.”
mn31:14.2: But for the transcending of this abiding and for the calming of this abiding, have you attained another comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state?”
mn31:14.4: Here, *bhante*, whenever we wish, with the fading of *pīti*, we dwell in equanimity [*upekkhakā*], with *sati*, fully aware [*sampajānā*], and we experience *sukha* with the body; we enter and dwell in the third *jhāna*, of which the noble ones declare: ‘One who is equanimous [*upekkhako*], with *sati*, dwells in *sukha*.’
mn31:14.5: For the transcending of this abiding and for the calming of this abiding, this is another comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state, that we have attained.”
mn31:15.2: But for the transcending of this abiding and for the calming of this abiding, have you attained another comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state?”
mn31:15.4: Here, *bhante*, whenever we wish, with the giving up of *sukha* and the giving up of *dukkha*, and with the previous disappearance of gladness and sadness, we enter and dwell in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither-painful-nor-pleasant and is purified by equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati* [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*].
mn31:15.5: For the transcending of this abiding and for the calming of this abiding, this is another comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state, that we have attained.”
mn31:16.2: But for the transcending of this abiding and for the calming of this abiding, have you attained another comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state?”
mn31:16.4: Here, *bhante*, whenever we wish, with the complete transcending of the *saññās* of form [*rūpasaññānaṁ*], with the disappearance of the *saññās* of resistance [*paṭighasaññānaṁ*], and through non-attention to the *saññās* of diversity [*nānattasaññānaṁ*], thinking, ‘Space is infinite,’ we enter and dwell in the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*].
mn31:16.5: For the transcending of this abiding and for the calming of this abiding, this is another comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state, that we have attained.”
mn31:17.2: But for the transcending of this abiding and for the calming of this abiding, have you attained another comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state?”
mn31:17.4: Here, *bhante*, whenever we wish, completely transcending the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘Consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] is infinite,’ we enter and dwell in the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*] …
mn31:17.5: completely transcending the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘There is nothing,’ we enter and dwell in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*] …
mn31:17.6: completely transcending the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*], we enter and dwell in the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*].
mn31:17.7: For the transcending of this abiding and for the calming of this abiding, this is another comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state, that we have attained.”
mn31:18.2: But for the transcending of this abiding and for the calming of this abiding, have you attained another comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state?”
mn31:18.4: Here, *bhante*, whenever we wish, completely transcending the dimension of neither-*saññā*-nor-non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*], we enter and dwell in the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*], and by seeing with discernment [*paññāya*], our *āsavas* are exhausted.
mn31:18.5: For the transcending of this abiding and for the calming of this abiding, this is another comfortable abiding, a further distinction in noble knowledge and vision beyond the human state, that we have attained.
mn31:18.6: And beyond this comfortable abiding, *bhante*, we do not see any other comfortable abiding that is more excellent or more sublime.”
mn31:18.8: Beyond this comfortable abiding there is no comfortable abiding more excellent or more sublime.”
mn31:19.1: Then the *Bhagavā*, having instructed, encouraged, inspired, and gladdened Venerable Anuruddha, Venerable Nandiya, and Venerable Kimbila with *dhamma* talk, rose from his seat and departed.
mn31:2.1: Now at that time Venerable Anuruddha, Venerable Nandiya, and Venerable Kimbila were dwelling in the Gosiṅga sāla-tree grove.
mn31:20.1: Then Venerable Anuruddha, Venerable Nandiya, and Venerable Kimbila accompanied the *Bhagavā* a little way, and after turning back from there, Venerable Nandiya and Venerable Kimbila said this to Venerable Anuruddha:
mn31:20.2: “But when did we ever tell Venerable Anuruddha this:
mn31:20.3: ‘We have attained these and these meditative attainments,’ that Venerable Anuruddha should declare in the presence of the *Bhagavā* even up to the destruction of the *āsavas*?”
mn31:20.5: ‘We have attained these and these meditative attainments’; rather, it was known by me through mind [*cetasā*], having encompassed the mind [*ceto*] of the venerables with mind [*cetasā*]:
mn31:20.6: ‘These venerables have attained these and these meditative attainments.’
mn31:20.7: And *devatās* too informed me of this matter:
mn31:20.8: ‘These venerables have attained these and these meditative attainments.’
mn31:20.9: Then, when questioned by the *Bhagavā*, I answered it.”
mn31:21.1: Then the yakkha Dīgha Parajana went to the *Bhagavā*; having approached, he paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and stood to one side. Standing to one side, the yakkha Dīgha Parajana said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn31:21.16: “It is a gain indeed, sirs, for the Vajjis, a great gain for the Vajji people,
mn31:21.17: where the *Tathāgata* dwells, the *arahant*, the perfectly awakened one, and these three clansmen—
mn31:21.18: Venerable Anuruddha, Venerable Nandiya, and Venerable Kimbila.”
mn31:21.19: Thus those venerables became known, at that very instant, at that very moment, as far as the Brahmā world.
mn31:21.2: “It is a gain indeed, *bhante*, for the Vajjis, a great gain for the Vajji people,
mn31:21.3: where the *Tathāgata* dwells, the *arahant*, the perfectly awakened one, and these three clansmen—
mn31:21.4: Venerable Anuruddha, Venerable Nandiya, and Venerable Kimbila.”
mn31:21.5: Hearing the sound of the yakkha Dīgha Parajana, the earth-dwelling *devatās* proclaimed the sound:
mn31:21.6: “It is a gain indeed, sirs, for the Vajjis, a great gain for the Vajji people,
mn31:21.7: where the *Tathāgata* dwells, the *arahant*, the perfectly awakened one, and these three clansmen—
mn31:21.8: Venerable Anuruddha, Venerable Nandiya, and Venerable Kimbila.”
mn31:21.9: Hearing the sound of the earth-dwelling *devatās*, the *devatās* of the Four Great Kings …
mn31:22.1: “That is so, Dīgha, that is so, Dīgha.
mn31:22.11: even if all workers, Dīgha, were to recollect these three clansmen with confident minds, that would be for the welfare and happiness of all those workers for a long time.
mn31:22.12: Even if the world with its *devatās*, with its Māras, with its Brahmās, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, with its *devatās* and humans, were to recollect these three clansmen with a confident mind, that would be for the welfare and happiness of the world with its *devatās*, with its Māras, with its Brahmās, of this population with its ascetics and brahmins, with its *devatās* and humans, for a long time.
mn31:22.13: See, Dīgha, how far these three clansmen are practicing for the welfare of the many, for the happiness of the many, out of compassion for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of *devatās* and humans.”
mn31:22.15: Satisfied, the yakkha Dīgha Parajana delighted in what the *Bhagavā* said.
mn31:22.16: The Shorter Discourse at Gosiṅga is finished, the first.
mn31:22.2: Whatever family, Dīgha, these three clansmen went forth from home to homelessness from, if that very family were to recollect these three clansmen with a confident mind, that would be for the welfare and happiness of that family for a long time.
mn31:22.3: Whatever family circle, Dīgha, these three clansmen went forth from home to homelessness from, if that very family circle were to recollect these three clansmen with a confident mind, that would be for the welfare and happiness of that family circle for a long time.
mn31:22.4: Whatever village, Dīgha, these three clansmen went forth from home to homelessness from, if that very village were to recollect these three clansmen with a confident mind, that would be for the welfare and happiness of that village for a long time.
mn31:22.5: Whatever market town, Dīgha, these three clansmen went forth from home to homelessness from, if that very market town were to recollect these three clansmen with a confident mind, that would be for the welfare and happiness of that market town for a long time.
mn31:22.6: Whatever city, Dīgha, these three clansmen went forth from home to homelessness from, if that very city were to recollect these three clansmen with a confident mind, that would be for the welfare and happiness of that city for a long time.
mn31:22.7: Whatever region, Dīgha, these three clansmen went forth from home to homelessness from, if that very region were to recollect these three clansmen with a confident mind, that would be for the welfare and happiness of that region for a long time.
mn31:22.8: Even if all nobles, Dīgha, were to recollect these three clansmen with confident minds, that would be for the welfare and happiness of all those nobles for a long time.
mn31:3.1: Then, in the evening, the *Bhagavā* emerged from retreat and went to the Gosiṅga sāla-tree grove.
mn31:3.2: The park-keeper saw the *Bhagavā* coming from afar.
mn31:3.5: There are three clansmen dwelling here who want to be left to themselves.
mn31:3.6: Do not make things uncomfortable for them.”
mn31:4.1: Venerable Anuruddha heard the park-keeper conversing with the *Bhagavā*.
mn31:4.2: Having heard, he said this to the park-keeper:
mn31:4.5: Then Venerable Anuruddha went to Venerable Nandiya and Venerable Kimbila, and on arrival he said this to them:
mn31:4.6: “Come forward, venerables, come forward, venerables; our Teacher, the *Bhagavā*, has arrived.”
mn31:5.1: Then Venerable Anuruddha, Venerable Nandiya, and Venerable Kimbila went out to meet the *Bhagavā*—
mn31:5.2: one received the *Bhagavā*’s bowl and robe, one prepared a seat, and one set out water for the feet.
mn31:5.3: The *Bhagavā* sat down on the seat that had been prepared.
mn31:5.4: After sitting down, the *Bhagavā* washed his feet.
mn31:5.5: Then those venerables paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side.
mn31:5.6: When Venerable Anuruddha was sitting to one side, the *Bhagavā* said this to him:
mn31:5.7: “Anuruddha, I hope things are bearable for you all, I hope you are getting by, I hope you are not wearied by almsfood?”
mn31:5.8: “Things are bearable, *Bhagavā*, we are getting by, *Bhagavā*; and, *bhante*, we are not wearied by almsfood.”
mn31:6.1: “But Anuruddha, I hope you are dwelling in concord, harmoniously, without disputes, blended like milk and water, looking upon one another with kindly eyes?”
mn31:6.2: “Indeed, *bhante*, we are dwelling in concord, harmoniously, without disputes, blended like milk and water, looking upon one another with kindly eyes.”
mn31:6.3: “But how, Anuruddha, are you dwelling in concord, harmoniously, without disputes, blended like milk and water, looking upon one another with kindly eyes?”
mn31:7.10: For our bodies are different, *bhante*, but I regard our mind [*cittaṁ*] as one.”
mn31:7.12: Venerable Kimbila too said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn31:7.14: ‘It is a gain for me indeed, it is well gained by me indeed,
mn31:7.15: that I dwell together with such fellow spiritual companions.’
mn31:7.16: For me, *bhante*, bodily action of goodwill [*mettaṁ kāyakammaṁ*] toward these venerables is established both openly and privately,
mn31:7.17: verbal action of goodwill [*mettaṁ vacīkammaṁ*] is established both openly and privately,
mn31:7.18: mental action of goodwill [*mettaṁ manokammaṁ*] is established both openly and privately.
mn31:7.2: ‘It is a gain for me indeed, it is well gained by me indeed,
mn31:7.20: ‘Why don’t I set aside my own mind [*cittaṁ*] and conduct myself according to the mind [*cittassa*] of just these venerables?’
mn31:7.21: So, *bhante*, I set aside my own mind [*cittaṁ*] and conduct myself according to the mind [*cittassa*] of just these venerables.
mn31:7.22: For our bodies are different, *bhante*, but I regard our mind [*cittaṁ*] as one.”
mn31:7.23: “This is how, *bhante*, we dwell in concord, harmoniously, without disputes, blended like milk and water, looking upon one another with kindly eyes.”
mn31:7.3: that I dwell together with such fellow spiritual companions.’
mn31:7.4: For me, *bhante*, bodily action of goodwill [*mettaṁ kāyakammaṁ*] toward these venerables is established both openly and privately;
mn31:7.5: verbal action of goodwill [*mettaṁ vacīkammaṁ*] is established both openly and privately;
mn31:7.6: mental action of goodwill [*mettaṁ manokammaṁ*] is established both openly and privately.
mn31:7.8: ‘Why don’t I set aside my own mind [*cittaṁ*] and conduct myself according to the mind [*cittassa*] of just these venerables?’
mn31:7.9: So, *bhante*, I set aside my own mind [*cittaṁ*] and conduct myself according to the mind [*cittassa*] of just these venerables.
mn31:8.2: But Anuruddha, I hope you are dwelling heedfully, ardently, and resolutely?”
mn31:9.1: “Indeed, *bhante*, we are dwelling heedfully, ardently, and resolutely.”
mn31:9.2: “But how, Anuruddha, are you dwelling heedfully, ardently, and resolutely?”
mn31:9.3: “Here, *bhante*, whichever of us returns first from almsround in the village prepares the seats, sets out drinking water and washing water, and sets out the refuse bowl.
mn31:9.4: Whoever returns later from almsround in the village, if there are leftovers and if he wishes, eats them; but if he does not wish, he throws them away where there is little greenery, or tips them into water without living beings.
mn31:9.5: He puts away the seats, puts away the drinking water and washing water, puts away the refuse bowl, and sweeps the refectory.
mn31:9.6: Whoever sees that the drinking-water jar, the washing-water jar, or the toilet-water jar is empty and vacant, fills it up.
mn31:9.7: If he is unable, he summons a second with a gesture of the hand, and together we fill it up by hand-signals; but, *bhante*, because of that we do not break into speech.
mn31:9.8: And every five nights, *bhante*, we sit together for the whole night in *dhamma* discussion.
mn31:9.9: This is how, *bhante*, we dwell heedfully, ardently, and resolutely.”
